content.title: Climate Change: Instant Expert
content.summary: {'A': '全球气候变化已经成为现实，不仅科学家通过树木年轮、古代珊瑚和冰芯中的气泡等证据看到了这一点，加拿大因纽特人、拉丁美洲和南亚的贫民窟居民以及欧洲人也通过消失的北极冰和永久冻土、致命的风暴和洪水、消失的冰川、森林火灾和致命的热浪等现象看到了气候变化的影响，而过去30年的全球变暖速度可能是前所未有的，这个时期本应由太阳周期和火山等自然因素降温。', 'B': '人类活动导致的全球变暖是由于燃烧大量的煤炭、石油和天然气释放了数十亿吨二氧化碳，尽管一些科学家认为这种变化实际上可能始于农业的兴起，而二氧化碳是一种温室气体，它与其他人为温室气体一起积累，如甲烷和氯氟烃，一些研究表明宇宙射线也可能与变暖有关。', 'C': '如果当前趋势持续下去，本世纪大气中的CO浓度将增加到工业化前水平的两倍，这可能足以使全球气温上升约2至5度，而这种升温程度将由融化冰川、海洋、水蒸气、云和植被变化等循环所决定。升温还带来了其他不可预测的变化，如冰川融化和降水导致一些河流泛滥，而蒸发则导致其他河流干涸，疾病也在传播，一些作物生长更快，而其他作物则因疾病和干旱而减产，水资源的减少可能导致许多地区发生冲突。', 'D': '自然生态系统的破坏导致生物多样性减少，大部分物种无法迁移适应，但有些物种已经在进化以应对气候变暖；海洋热膨胀和陆地冰融化也导致海平面上升，人类活动可能引发格陵兰冰盖的不可逆融化，使全球海平面上升六米，淹没数十亿人类居住的土地。', 'E': '全球变暖的速度会更加明显，如果不是因为硫磺颗粒和其他污染物的遮蔽作用，以及森林和海洋吸收我们产生的二氧化碳的能力，但自2001年以来，大气中二氧化碳的积累速率已经翻倍，表明自然界吸收这种气体的能力可能已经达到了极限，最近的研究表明，像泥炭沼泽和森林这样的自然二氧化碳“汇”实际上开始释放二氧化碳。', 'F': '1992年地球峰会上，世界同意防止“危险”的气候变化，第一步是1997年生效的《京都议定书》，它将带来工业化国家的适度减排，但许多观察家认为需要更深的减排，并且人口众多且不断增长的发展中国家将来也必须加入，大多数科学家认为我们低估了危险。', 'G': '总结：根据IPCC的报告，为了稳定大气中的二氧化碳浓度和温度，世界需要迅速提高能源利用效率，发展风能、太阳能、潮汐能、波浪能和可能的核能等可再生非碳燃料，并开发将清洁能源转化为动力的新方法，如用于汽车的氢燃料电池，同时还需要采取其他非常规的解决方案，如通过巨大的镜子反射太阳光线、在海洋中撒铁以产生藻类大量繁殖，或将温室气体埋藏在海底等。最重要的是，我们需要将二氧化碳排放量削减70%至80%，以稳定大气中的二氧化碳浓度和温度，而且我们越快做到这一点，未来世界的温度就会越不难以忍受。'}

paragraphs.label: A
paragraphs.text: Climate change is with us. A decade ago, it was conjecture. Now the future is unfolding before our eyes. Canada's Inuit see it in disappearing Arctic ice and permafrost. The shantytown dwellers of Latin America and Southern Asia see it in lethal storms and floods. Europeans see it in disappearing glaciers, forest fires and fatal heat waves. Scientists see it in tree rings, ancient coral and bubbles trapped in ice cores. These reveal that the world has not been as warm as it is now for a millennium or more. The three warmest years on record have all occurred since 1998; 19 of the warmest 20 since 1980. And Earth has probably never warmed as fast as in the past 30 years--a period when natural influences on global temperatures, such as solar cycles and volcanoes should have cooled us down.

paragraphs.label: B
paragraphs.text: Climatologists reporting for the UN Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) say we are seeing global warming caused by human activities. People are causing the change by burning nature's vast stores of coal, oil and natural gas. This releases billions of tonnes of carbon dioxide (CO ) every year, although the changes may actually have started with the dawn of agriculture, 2 say some scientists. The physics of the "greenhouse effect" has been a matter of scientific fact for a century. CO is a greenhouse gas that traps the Sun's radiation within the troposphere, the lower atmosphere. 2 It has accumulated along with other manmade greenhouse gases, such as methane and chlorofluorocarbons (CFCs). Some studies suggest that cosmic rays may also be involved in warming.

paragraphs.label: C
paragraphs.text: . If current trends continue, we will raise atmospheric CO concentrations to double pre-industrial levels during this century. That will probably be enough 2 to raise global temperatures by around 2 to 5. Some warming is certain, but the degree will be determined by cycles involving melting ice, the oceans, water vapour, clouds and changes to vegetation. Warming is bringing other unpredictable changes. Melting glaciers and precipitation are causing some rivers to overflow, while evaporation is emptying others. Diseases are spreading. Some crops grow faster while others see yields slashed by disease and drought. Clashes over dwindling water resources may cause conflicts in many regions.

paragraphs.label: D
paragraphs.text: As natural ecosystems - such as coral reefs - are disrupted, biodiversity is reduced. Most species cannot migrate fast enough to keep up, though others are already evolving in response to warming. Thermal expansion of the oceans, combined with melting ice on land, is also raising sea levels. In this century, human activity could trigger an irreversible melting of the Greenland ice sheet. This would condemn the world to a rise in sea level of six metres - enough to flood land occupied by billions of people.

paragraphs.label: E
paragraphs.text:
The global warming would be more pronounced if it were not for sulphur particles and other pollutants that shade us, and because forests and oceans absorb around half of the CO we produce. But the accumulation rate of atmospheric CO has doubled since 2001, suggesting 2 that nature's ability to absorb the gas could now be 2 stretched to the limit. Recent research suggests that natural CO "sinks", like peat bogs and forests, are actually starting to release 2
CO . 2

paragraphs.label: F
paragraphs.text: At the Earth Summit in 1992, the world agreed to prevent "dangerous" climate change. The first step was the 1997 Kyoto Protocol, which came into force during 2005. It will bring modest emission reductions from industrialised countries. Many observers say deeper cuts are needed and developing nations, which have large and growing populations, will one day have to join in. Some, including the US Bush administration, say the scientific uncertainty over the pace of climate change is grounds for delaying action. The US and Australia have reneged on Kyoto. Most scientists believe we are under-estimating the dangers.

paragraphs.label: G
paragraphs.text: In any case, according to the IPCC, the world needs to quickly improve the efficiency of its energy usage and develop renewable non-carbon fuels like: wind, solar, tidal, wave and perhaps nuclear power. It also means developing new methods of converting this clean energy into motive power, like hydrogen fuel cells for cars. Other less conventional solutions include ideas to stave off warming by "mega-engineering" the planet with giant mirrors to deflect the Sun's rays, seeding the oceans with iron to generate algal blooms, or burying greenhouse gases below the sea. The bottom line is that we will need to cut CO emissions by 70% to 80% simply to stabilise atmospheric CO concentrations--and thus 2 temperatures. The quicker we do that, the less unbearably hot our 2 future world will be.

content.title: Britain's canals - the solution to overcrowded roads?
content.summary: {'A': '这段话主要讲述了过去十年左右国家的运河曾经是被废弃的购物车堆积的地方，但现在已经发生了巨大的转变，成为城市中心的时尚地标，并且被认为是解决交通拥堵问题的重要途径。', 'B': '欧洲热衷于发展内陆水路运输，因为货运量预计在未来十年将增长三分之一，而污染和拥堵的成本将在2010年占据欧洲整体GDP的1%。', 'C': '欧洲大陆的运河和河流在英国工业选择铁路和公路之后仍然保持繁忙，而英国的运河则在其鼎盛时期仅持续了60年，150年前就被认为过时，许多运河被遗弃。相比之下，许多欧洲国家在100年前甚至扩建了运河。', 'D': '英国的水路在休闲者和工业的推动下逆转了衰退，现在船家、钓鱼者、徒步者和骑行者都能享受到约4000英里的可通航水道以及沿岸的路径和小道，水边生活变得时尚，城市中心的运河成为城市更新的焦点，尽管我们对道路交通有着固执的追求，但环境考虑正在迫使政府和企业将时钟拨回200年前，至少在某种程度上重新启用我们的水路。', 'E': '这段话主要讲述了工业品如煤炭、钢铁、骨料和石油从未完全消失于大型河流和商业航道，水上运输货物的能源消耗仅为道路运输的四分之一，对比卡车，驳船排放低、噪音低且视觉上不突兀，因此运用运河运输货物可以减少污染和气候变化。', 'F': '运河的承载能力有限，水上交通的缓慢速度与新鲜农产品有限的保质期不相符合，因此一些超市可能会选择将保护农产品的废纸和塑料运输到水上，而许多利用英国水路的提案都是围绕废物管理和回收计划展开的，例如在伦敦东部的哈克尼区进行的试点计划，将市政废物由卡车收集并转运到再处理厂，未来该计划每年可减少该区30万英里的垃圾车行驶里程，目前的安排可能只是冰山一角。', 'G': '威尔士登交汇处的Powerday回收厂获得了规划许可，该厂将处理每年150万吨的废物，但公路运输的废物数量将被限制在50万吨，如果他们想发展业务，就必须与我们合作。', 'H': '尽管细节尚未确定，英国水道公司认为，将一些用于伦敦巨型奥运建设项目的建筑材料通过连接泰晤士河和东伦敦的水道网络进行运输是最合适的方式。'}

paragraphs.label: A
paragraphs.text: It's hard to imagine that only a decade or so ago many of the nation's canals were little more than the last resting place for abandoned shopping trolleys. There's still work to be done, but their transformation has been remarkable. Projects such as Castlefield in Manchester and Brindley Place in Birmingham have transformed city-centre canals from stagnant reminders of a fading industrial past to the epitome of urban cool. However, 21st-century priorities dictate that the rehabilitation of this 18th-century motorway system cannot stop there. Canals and navigable rivers form a major transport network, in need of only piecemeal investment, and with the spare capacity to take away the need for hundreds of thousands of lorry journeys. In the second half of the 18th century, canals drove the industrial revolution. Today, authorities want them to drive congestion off the roads. Last month, for example, the European Commission proposed a seven-year plan to shift large amounts of freight from roads to inland waterways.

paragraphs.label: B
paragraphs.text: Europe's enthusiasm comes as no surprise. Freight traffic is expected to grow by a third in the next decade. The cost of pollution and congestion is set to swallow one per cent of Europe's entire GDP by 2010. "With a fleet of 11000 vessels and a capacity equalling 10000 trains or 440000 trucks, inland waterways can make transport in Europe more efficient, reliable and environmental friendly," says Jacques Barrot, vice-president of the European Commission in charge of transport. "Europe cannot afford to leave that potential untapped."

paragraphs.label: C
paragraphs.text: Mainland Europe has never, in fairness, left it completely untapped. The canals of the low countries and the rivers of central and eastern Europe buzzed with the sound of freight barges long after British industry had thrown in its lot with railways and roads. Attempts to revive freight on British canals have been hampered by the fact that their heyday lasted barely 60 years, and they were first considered obsolete 150 years ago. For much of the intervening period, many have simply been left to rot. "Our network was in decline for a long time compared to much of Europe," says Eugene Baston of British Waterways. "Whereas other countries developed road and rail transport but carried on using their waterways as well, our canals were neglected. In fact many European countries actually enlarged their canals 100 years ago."

paragraphs.label: D
paragraphs.text: That decline in Britain has been reversed, first by leisure seekers and more recently by industry. Boaters, anglers, walkers and cyclists now benefit from around 4000 miles of navigable waterways and the paths and trails that run alongside them. Waterside living is fashionable, and city-centre canals have been a focus for urban renewal, And, despite our obsession with road transport, environmental considerations are forcing government and business to mm the clock back 200 years and--at least in a minor way--get our waterways working again.

paragraphs.label: E
paragraphs.text: In fact, industrial goods such as coal, steel, aggregates and petroleum have never completely disappeared from large rivers and designated commercial waterways. Barges on the river Severn have recently started carrying the equivalent of 34000 lorry loads of aggregates each year, the first freight traffic on the river for a decade. British Waterways, which owns about half of the country's navigable inland waterways, carded the equivalent of 64000 25-ton lorry loads of freight in 2004. The organisation says these figures are certain to increase as new schemes start, and environmentalists hope they will. Carrying freight by water uses about a quarter of the energy of an equivalent road journey. In comparison to lorries, barges produce low emissions, low noise and are visually unobtrusive. "We think that anything that can take freight off the roads needs to be fully explored," says Tony Bosworth, transport campaigner for Friends of the Earth. "Canals can do that. They can help cut the pollution that causes climate change."

paragraphs.label: F
paragraphs.text: There is a limit to what canals can carry. The slow pace of water travel does not fit well with the limited shelf-life of fresh produce. If supermarkets won't trust their cherry tomatoes to water, they might trust the waste paper and plastic that protects them. Many of the proposals to utilise Britain's waterways are based around waste management and recycling schemes. For example, a pilot scheme in Hackney, east London, has seen municipal waste collected by truck and transferred to barge for transportation to a reprocessing plant. In the future, the scheme could remove 300000 dustcart miles from the borough's streets every year. Current arrangements could be just the tip of the iceberg.

paragraphs.label: G
paragraphs.text: Planning permission has been given for a Powerday recycling plant at Willesden Junction, a site that sits on the intersection of road, rail and canal networks. "The plant will have the capacity to handle a million and a half tons of waste every year, but the amount carried by road will be capped at 500000 tons," says Ed Fox of British Waterways London. "If they want to grow the business, they will have to work with us." Fox says getting freight back on the canals has been "a nice idea" for 50 years, but until recently little more than an idea. "The Powerday project, on the other hand, is proof of what really can be done."

paragraphs.label: H
paragraphs.text: Though details have yet to be decided, British Waterways believes the most appropriate way to transport some of the building materials destined for London's giant Olympic construction project is by the network of waterways that links the Thames and east London. The Olympic Delivery Authority says: "It's being looked at and the final solution could well involve some transportation by water. What exactly we do will be based on a range of factors, but one of those will be sustainability." Their gentle pace will always make canals a niche player in a busy world, but after 200 years of neglect, the tide is starting to turn.

paragraphs.label: A
paragraphs.text: The world's factory, it turns out, has a sizeable canteen attached, not to mention an office block and shopping mall. Last month's official revision of China's gross domestic product revealed an economy worth 16 trillion yuan (�1.9 trillion) in 2004, 17% more than previously thought. Some�265 billion of the increase - 93% of it - was ascribed to the services sector. As a result,services' share of the economy has jumped by nine percentage points, to 41%, compared with 46% for manufacturing and 13% for primary industries (mainly agriculture and mining).

paragraphs.label: B
paragraphs.text: Where has all this extra activity come from? The bulk of it is obvious to any traveller in China. As people grow wealthier, they want more restaurants and bars, clothes stores, car dealerships, bookshops, private hospitals, English language classes and beauty salons. In many of these businesses, however, turnover and profits have not previously been captured by a statistical system geared to measuring factory production. The small, often private, companies that dominate these areas have also often been at pains to escape notice - and therefore taxes.

paragraphs.label: C
paragraphs.text: Li Deshui, commissioner of China's National Bureau of Statistics, confirms that most of the newly unearthed GDP comes from three categories. The first is wholesale, retail and catering; the second, transport, storage, post and telecommunications. While postal and telecoms services are still state-controlled and thus readily measured, more than a million small tracking and removal companies are not. The third activity is real estate, booming particularly in the coastal cities and increasingly inland too, leading to an influx of private money - not least from overseas speculators. Property development has, in turn, boosted demand for architects, decorators, do-it-yourself stores and other building services.

paragraphs.label: D
paragraphs.text: There is more to China's services boom than dishing up stir-fries, shipping boxes and fitting out apartments. Recent years have seen a surge in media and technology services, including the internet; in financial services such as leasing; and in education and leisure. In a small way, for example, China is starting to rival India as an outsourcing hub: less for call-centres that require excellent English than for such tasks as preparing reports and patent filings. In October Microsoft took a stake in a Chinese software firm in Dalian, a city in north-east China with a thriving outsourcing industry preparing tax returns and software for companies from Japan and South Korea.

paragraphs.label: E
paragraphs.text: China's rapid economic growth is fuelling demand for accountants, lawyers, bankers and all manner of consultants, as Chinese companies expand and restructure. Specialists in marketing, advertising and public relations advise on the relatively new area of marketing products and developing brands. The new wealth has other consequences, too. China now has nearly a million security guards. It can offer its new rich everything from cosmetic surgeons to pet salons.

paragraphs.label: F
paragraphs.text: Meanwhile, a huge new market is opening up for private education--fuelled by the combination of a poor public system, the preoccupation of middle-class parents with giving their (often) only child the best chances, and demand from business. Chinese families spend more on education than on anything except housing - the market for courses, books and materials more than doubled from 2002 levels, to �90 billion in 2005. Richer households have also caused a tourism boom, which is still chiefly domestic, though more mainlanders are venturing overseas as visa restrictions are lifted. The World Travel & Tourism Council predicts that China's annual tourism market will more than triple to �300 billion within a decade.

paragraphs.label: G
paragraphs.text: China's services sector, on this basis, is well-developed and roughly as large as those of Japan and South Korea were at a similar stage of development, notes the HSBC bank. In reality, it is bigger still, since the GDP revision cannot capture activities such as kerbside lending and tax-dodging cash transactions in property or entertainment--all of which Dong Tao, chief Asia economist at CSFB, another bank, reckons add another �220 billion to the economy. Even so, the 41% of GDP claimed by services in China remains below the 60-75% typical in developed countries. It is smaller even than India's 52%.

paragraphs.label: H
paragraphs.text: One reason for this is a bias towards manufacturing--"China's 'real-men-make-stuff' attitude,"as Gordon Orr of McKinsey's Shanghai office puts it. This has led to a plethora of ill-thought-through regulations for services, made worse by China's continuing suspicion of private business, which is mostly concentrated in the services sector. The lack of a national trucking licence, for example, means hauliers must get approval from each province to move goods across the country and unload them on to different trucks at each border--delaying delivery and increasing spoilage and pilfering. In retailing, local governments often maintain inefficient supply chains, in part to protect local jobs. David Wei, head of B&Q in China, says his 48 do-it-yourself stores on the mainland are served by 1800 suppliers, compared with 600 suppliers in Britain for more than 300 stores.

paragraphs.label: I
paragraphs.text: Worse, though China took an early decision to invite foreign direct investment into manufacturing, it has been reluctant to open up services. Diana Farrell, director of the McKinsey Global Institute, the consultancy's think-tank, argues that allowing more foreign investment in services could bring not just capital and technology but a competitive dynamic. The presence of Carrefour and Wal-Mart has led to domestic copycats, creating innovation and productivity growth.

在这道题中，我们需要填入一个词来完成句子。通过定位词"China"，我们可以在原文中找到相关信息。原文中提到，尽管中国早早决定邀请外国直接投资到制造业，但对于开放服务业却持保留态度。这表明中国在服务业方面的发展相对较慢。因此，我们可以推断出填入的词应该与服务业的发展有关。

接下来，我们需要找到一个与"invite"相同意的词，以及一个与"reluctant"相同意的词。在原文中，"invite"被同义词"allowing more foreign investment"替代，而"reluctant"被同义词"has been reluctant to open up"替代。这些同义表达所在的句子是："Worse, though China took an early decision to invite foreign direct investment into manufacturing, it has been reluctant to open up services."

最后，我们需要找到一个与"services"相同意的词。在原文中，"services"被同义词"more foreign investment in services"替代。这个同义表达所在的句子是："Diana Farrell, director of the McKinsey Global Institute, the consultancy's think-tank, argues that allowing more foreign investment in services could bring not just capital and technology but a competitive dynamic."

综上所述，根据原文中的信息，我们可以得出答案为"foreign direct investment"。

central_sentences.sentence: Worse, though China took an early decision to invite foreign direct investment into manufacturing, it has been reluctant to open up services
central_sentences.translation: 更糟糕的是，尽管中国早早决定邀请外国直接投资制造业，但对于开放服务行业却一直持保留态度。

Worse, though China took an early decision to invite foreign direct investment into manufacturing, it has been reluctant to open up services.

groups.desc:
Questions 36-40
Do the following statements agree with the information given in Reading Passage 3? 
In boxes 36-40 choose

questions.title: Officially, the largest sector in China is the service sector.
questions.analysis: 题干说中国的最大部门是服务业。我们可以在原文中找到相关信息。原文中提到，服务业在经济中的份额增加了9个百分点，达到41%，而制造业占46%，初级产业（主要是农业和矿业）占13%。所以，服务业并不是中国的最大部门，因此答案是"FALSE"。

central_sentences.sentence: As a result,services' share of the economy has jumped by nine percentage points, to 41%, compared with 46% for manufacturing and 13% for primary industries (mainly agriculture and mining
central_sentences.translation: 结果是，与制造业的46%和主要产业（主要是农业和矿业）的13%相比，服务业在经济中的份额增加了9个百分点，达到了41%。

target_sen.target_sen: As a result,services' share of the economy has jumped by nine percentage points, to 41%, compared with 46% for manufacturing and 13% for primary industries (mainly agriculture and mining).
Last month's official revision of China's gross domestic product revealed an economy worth 16 trillion yuan (�1.9 trillion) in 2004, 17% more than previously thought.
Some�265 billion of the increase - 93% of it - was ascribed to the services sector.

questions.title: Some of the newly-discovered GDP comes from the education sector.
questions.analysis: 题干中提到了"newly-discovered GDP"和"education sector"。我们可以在原文中找到同义表达，即"newly unearthed GDP"和"private education"。然而，原文中并没有明确提到这些新发现的GDP来自教育领域。因此，根据题干中的信息，我们无法确定这些新发现的GDP是否来自教育领域。所以，答案是"NOT GIVEN"。

central_sentences.sentence: Li Deshui, commissioner of China's National Bureau of Statistics, confirms that most of the newly unearthed GDP comes from three categories
central_sentences.translation: 中国国家统计局局长李德水确认，新发现的大部分国内生产总值（GDP）来自三个类别。

central_sentences.sentence: Meanwhile, a huge new market is opening up for private education--fuelled by the combination of a poor public system, the preoccupation of middle-class parents with giving their (often) only child the best chances, and demand from business. Chinese families spend more on education than on anything except housing - the market for courses, books and materials more than doubled from 2002 levels, to �90 billion in 2005
central_sentences.translation: 与此同时，一个巨大的私人教育市场正在兴起，这是由公共教育体系的糟糕状况、中产阶级家长为了给他们（通常是）唯一的孩子提供最好机会的关注以及商业需求共同推动的。中国家庭在教育上的支出仅次于住房 - 从2002年的水平翻了一番，到2005年达到了900亿。

target_sen.target_sen: Li Deshui, commissioner of China's National Bureau of Statistics, confirms that most of the newly unearthed GDP comes from three categories.

Meanwhile, a huge new market is opening up for private education--fuelled by the combination of a poor public system, the preoccupation of middle-class parents with giving their (often) only child the best chances, and demand from business.

syn_ary.synonym: market for courses, books and materials

target_sen.target_sen: Chinese families spend more on education than on anything except housing - the market for courses, books and materials more than doubled from 2002 levels, to �90 billion in 2005.

questions.title: Dalian is a successful outsourcing center for Japan and Korea.
questions.analysis: 根据题干"Dalian is a successful outsourcing center for Japan and Korea."，我们可以在原文中找到相关信息。原文中提到了Dalian这个城市，它是中国东北部的一个城市，拥有繁荣的外包产业，为来自日本和韩国的公司准备税务申报和软件。因此，根据原文中的信息，我们可以得出结论，Dalian是一个成功的外包中心，为日本和韩国提供服务。所以答案是"TRUE"。

central_sentences.sentence: In October Microsoft took a stake in a Chinese software firm in Dalian, a city in north-east China with a thriving outsourcing industry preparing tax returns and software for companies from Japan and South Korea
central_sentences.translation: 今年十月，微软在中国大连市投资了一家软件公司。大连市位于中国东北部，拥有繁荣的外包行业，为来自日本和韩国的公司准备税务申报和软件。

questions.title: As visa restrictions are lowered, Chinese people are expected to spend more than�300 billion on tourism.
questions.analysis: 题干中提到中国人预计在旅游上花费超过3000亿。我们可以在原文中找到相关信息。原文中提到富裕家庭引起了旅游热潮，尽管签证限制放宽，但主要还是国内旅游，不过越来越多的大陆人开始涉足海外。而世界旅游与旅游理事会预测，中国的年度旅游市场将在十年内增长到3000亿。所以，根据原文中的信息，我们可以得出结论，中国人在旅游上的花费将超过3000亿。因此，答案是"TRUE"。

central_sentences.sentence: Richer households have also caused a tourism boom, which is still chiefly domestic, though more mainlanders are venturing overseas as visa restrictions are lifted. The World Travel & Tourism Council predicts that China's annual tourism market will more than triple to �300 billion within a decade
central_sentences.translation: 富裕家庭也引发了旅游业的繁荣，尽管更多的内地人在签证限制放宽后开始涉足海外，但旅游业仍然主要以国内为主。世界旅游与旅行理事会预测，中国每年的旅游市场规模将在十年内增长至3000亿美元以上。

questions.title: China's services sector is about the same size as Japan's and South Korea's.
questions.analysis: 题干中提到中国的服务业规模与日本和韩国相当。我们可以在原文中找到相关信息。原文中提到中国的服务业在发展阶段与日本和韩国相当，并且规模大于日本和韩国。所以，题干中的信息在原文中是可以找到的。因此，答案为"FALSE"。

central_sentences.sentence: China's services sector, on this basis, is well-developed and roughly as large as those of Japan and South Korea were at a similar stage of development, notes the HSBC bank
central_sentences.translation: 中国的服务业在这个基础上发展得很好，大致上与日本和韩国在类似发展阶段时的规模相当，汇丰银行指出。
